result:
:::writing
On the morning the sky forgot its color, everyone pretended not to notice.

It wasn’t dramatic at first. The blue simply thinned, like a shirt washed too many times, until it became something between memory and absence. People stepped out of their homes with coffee in their hands and schedules in their heads, glanced upward, and then chose—deliberately, almost politely—to look away.

By noon, the sky had settled into a flat, unmoving gray that didn’t shift with the sun. Shadows dulled. Edges softened. The world looked as if it had been copied one too many times, the contrast fading with each duplication.

A boy named Eli noticed.

He was nine, and he had a habit of noticing things that didn’t belong to anyone else. The way ants would reroute themselves around a crumb that wasn’t there the day before. The sound of his mother’s voice when she spoke on the phone to people she didn’t like. The exact moment a breeze decided to exist.

He stood in the backyard, toes curled into the cool grass, and stared up until his eyes watered.

“It’s wrong,” he said.

No one answered.

His mother was inside, stirring a pot of something that smelled like cumin and garlic. The radio murmured about traffic and a minor sports scandal. The neighbors’ dog barked at nothing in particular. The world continued, steady and unquestioning, as if the sky had always been this way.

Eli went inside.

“The sky is broken,” he told his mother.

She didn’t look up. “It’s just overcast, honey.”

“No, it’s not. It’s… gone.”

She tasted the soup, frowned, added salt. “Eat your lunch.”

He did, because hunger is a louder truth than most other things. But he kept glancing toward the window, half-expecting the gray to peel away like a sticker and reveal something underneath.

It didn’t.

By evening, people began to comment on it, but only in passing.

“Strange weather.”

“Feels heavy, doesn’t it?”

“Probably rain later.”

No one said what it felt like: that the sky had stopped being a place and become a ceiling.

Eli lay in bed that night and tried to remember what blue looked like. He knew the word. He knew it was his favorite color. But when he closed his eyes, he could only summon approximations—fragments of crayons worn down to stubs, the faded ink of an old shirt, a memory of something brighter that refused to take shape.

He sat up.

If the sky was gone, then where had it gone to?

This seemed like a reasonable question, the kind adults would ask if they were paying attention. Eli slid out of bed, careful not to wake his mother, and padded to the back door. The night outside was not dark in the usual way. It was a uniform dimness, like a room with all the lights turned off except one hidden in a corner.

He stepped into the yard.

“Hello?” he said.

He didn’t expect an answer. He wasn’t even sure who he was asking. But the word felt necessary, like knocking on a door before opening it.

For a moment, nothing happened.

Then the gray shifted.

Not dramatically—no tearing or flashing—but a subtle rearrangement, like fabric being adjusted. A faint ripple passed overhead, and Eli felt, rather than saw, something vast and patient turn its attention toward him.

“Oh,” he said.

It was the kind of “oh” that meant he understood less than before, but in a way that felt important.

“You’re not gone,” he said carefully. “You’re just… different.”

The gray seemed to deepen, as if acknowledging the thought.

“Why?” Eli asked.

The answer did not come as words. It came as a feeling, slow and layered: of exhaustion, of being looked at but not seen, of holding colors that no one bothered to notice anymore. It was the sensation of a thousand sunsets ignored, of stars drowned out by brighter, closer lights, of clouds reduced to background noise in photographs of other things.

Eli frowned.

“That’s not fair,” he said. “I look at you.”

The feeling shifted, just slightly. Curious now.

“I do,” he insisted. “I like the way you change. And the colors. And the… the big-ness.”

The gray above him quivered, like a breath held and then released.

A thin line appeared.

It was so faint Eli thought at first it was a trick of his eyes. But it grew, slowly, stretching across the sky in a pale arc. And within that line, there was color—not the full, bold blue he half-remembered, but a hint of it, like a whisper.

Eli grinned.

“There you are.”

The line widened, cautiously, as if testing whether it was welcome. A soft blue seeped into the gray, hesitant but undeniable. It wasn’t much. Most of the sky remained dim and muted. But the color was real, and it was enough to make Eli’s chest feel too small for his excitement.

“See?” he said. “You can come back.”

The feeling that followed was complicated. Not quite happiness. Not quite relief. Something quieter, steadier. Like the first step after standing still for too long.

The blue did not spread further that night. It held its ground, a narrow band cutting through the gray. But it stayed.

Eli went back inside, climbed into bed, and fell asleep with the image of it behind his eyelids—a promise more than a picture.

In the morning, people noticed.

“Looks like it’s clearing up.”

“About time.”

“Still kind of dull, though.”

They went about their days, as they always did. But some of them glanced upward a little longer than usual. Some of them paused, mid-step, as if trying to remember something just out of reach.

And in a backyard, a boy stood barefoot in the grass, looking up with the kind of attention that makes things real.

The sky, for its part, watched back.

Not fully restored. Not yet. But no longer entirely absent.

Sometimes, that’s how change begins—not with a dramatic return, but with a thin line of color that refuses to disappear, no matter how easy it would be to ignore.

And sometimes, that’s enough.
:::writing

:::writing

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
